flashcards.back: The process used to determine which device will become the bus master when multiple devices request control of the bus simultaneously.

flashcards.front: What is the main difference between centralized and distributed arbitration?
flashcards.back: Centralized arbitration uses a single hardware device (an arbiter) to manage requests, while distributed arbitration allows each device to decide priority amongst themselves without a central controller.

flashcards.front: What are the two main types of centralized arbitration schemes?
flashcards.back: 1. Daisy-chain arbitration (priority is set by physical position on the bus). 2. Independent-request arbitration (each device has a direct request line to the arbiter).

flashcards.front: In a daisy-chain arbitration scheme, which device has the highest priority?
flashcards.back: The device closest to the bus arbiter (or controller) has the highest priority, as the 'bus grant' signal propagates down the chain and is captured by the first device that requested it.

flashcards.front: What is a key disadvantage of the daisy-chain (polling) method of arbitration?
flashcards.back: It has a fixed priority structure. A single, low-priority device may be 'starved' (never gain bus access) if higher-priority devices constantly make requests.
